content:
# Genesis Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 7r-7v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_012_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_012_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 8-9 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0012

---

*The Call of Abram (vv. 1–3)*

**12:1** And the LORD said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם]: Go for yourself [לֶךְ-לְךָ] from your land [מֵאַרְצְךָ] and from your kindred [וּמִמּוֹלַדְתְּךָ] and from the house of your father [וּמִבֵּית אָבִיךָ] to the land [אֶל-הָאָרֶץ] that I will show you [אֲשֶׁר אַרְאֶךָּ].

**12:2** And I will make you [וְאֶעֶשְׂךָ] into a great nation [לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל] and I will bless you [וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ] and I will make great your name [וַאֲגַדְּלָה שְׁמֶךָ] and be [וֶהְיֵה] a blessing [בְּרָכָה].

**12:3** And I will bless [וַאֲבָרְכָה] those who bless you [מְבָרְכֶיךָ] and the one who curses you [וּמְקַלֶּלְךָ] I will curse [אָאֹר]; and in you [וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ] all the families of the earth [כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה] shall bless themselves [וְנִבְרְכוּ].

---

*The Departure from Haran (vv. 4–9)*

**12:4** And Abram went [וַיֵּלֶךְ אַבְרָם] as the LORD had spoken to him [כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֵלָיו יְהוָה] and Lot went with him [וַיֵּלֶךְ אִתּוֹ לוֹט]; and Abram was [וְאַבְרָם] seventy-five years old [בֶּן-חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וְשִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה] when he went out [בְּצֵאתוֹ] from Haran [מֵחָרָן].

**12:5** And Abram took [וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָם] Sarai his wife [אֶת-שָׂרַי אִשְׁתּוֹ] and Lot son of his brother [וְאֶת-לוֹט בֶּן-אָחִיו] and all their possessions that they had acquired [וְאֶת-כָּל-רְכוּשָׁם אֲשֶׁר רָכָשׁוּ] and the persons [וְאֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ] whom they had made [אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ] in Haran [בְחָרָן] and they went forth [וַיֵּצְאוּ] to go to the land of Canaan [לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן] and they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to the land of Canaan [אַרְצָה כְּנָעַן].

**12:6** And Abram passed through [וַיַּעֲבֹר אַבְרָם] the land [בָּאָרֶץ] as far as the place of Shechem [עַד מְקוֹם שְׁכֶם] as far as the oak of Moreh [עַד אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה]; and the Canaanite [וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי] was then in the land [אָז בָּאָרֶץ].

**12:7** And the LORD appeared [וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה] to Abram [אֶל-אַבְרָם] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: To your seed [לְזַרְעֲךָ] I will give [אֶתֵּן] this land [אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת]; and he built there [וַיִּבֶן שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] to the LORD [לַיהוָה] who had appeared to him [הַנִּרְאֶה אֵלָיו].

**12:8** And he moved on [וַיַּעְתֵּק] from there [מִשָּׁם] to the mountain [הָהָרָה] on the east of Bethel [מִקֶּדֶם לְבֵית-אֵל] and he pitched his tent [וַיֶּט-אָהֳלֹה] with Bethel to the west [בֵּית-אֵל מִיָּם] and Ai to the east [וְהָעַי מִקֶּדֶם] and he built there [וַיִּבֶן-שָׁם] an altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] to the LORD [לַיהוָה] and called on the name of the LORD [וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה].

**12:9** And Abram journeyed on [וַיִּסַּע אַבְרָם] going and journeying [הָלוֹךְ וְנָסוֹעַ] toward the Negev [הַנֶּגְבָּה].

---

*Abram in Egypt: the Wife-Sister Deception (vv. 10–20)*

**12:10** And there was a famine [וַיְהִי רָעָב] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ] and Abram went down [וַיֵּרֶד אַבְרָם] to Egypt [מִצְרַיְמָה] to sojourn there [לָגוּר שָׁם] because [כִּי-כָבֵד] the famine was heavy [הָרָעָב] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ].

**12:11** And it was [וַיְהִי] as he drew near [כַּאֲשֶׁר הִקְרִיב] to enter Egypt [לָבוֹא מִצְרָיְמָה] that he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to Sarai his wife [אֶל-שָׂרַי אִשְׁתּוֹ]: Look [הִנֵּה-נָא], I know [יָדַעְתִּי] that you are a woman [כִּי אִשָּׁה] beautiful in appearance [יְפַת-מַרְאֶה אָתְּ].

**12:12** And it shall be [וְהָיָה] when the Egyptians see you [כִּי-יִרְאוּ אֹתָךְ הַמִּצְרִים] that they will say [וְאָמְרוּ]: This is his wife [אִשְׁתּוֹ זֹאת] and they will kill me [וְהָרְגוּ אֹתִי] and you they will keep alive [וְאֹתָךְ יְחַיּוּ].

**12:13** Say, please [אִמְרִי-נָא], you are my sister [אֲחֹתִי אָתְּ] so that it may go well with me [לְמַעַן יִיטַב-לִי] on account of you [בַּעֲבוּרֵךְ] and my life shall live [וְחָיְתָה נַפְשִׁי] because of you [בִּגְלָלֵךְ].

**12:14** And it was [וַיְהִי] when Abram entered [כְּבוֹא אַבְרָם] Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה] that the Egyptians saw [וַיִּרְאוּ הַמִּצְרִים] the woman [אֶת-הָאִשָּׁה] that she was very beautiful [כִּי-יָפָה הִוא מְאֹד].

**12:15** And the princes of Pharaoh saw her [וַיִּרְאוּ אֹתָהּ שָׂרֵי פַרְעֹה] and praised her [וַיְהַלְלוּ אֹתָהּ] before Pharaoh [אֶל-פַּרְעֹה] and the woman was taken [וַתֻּקַּח הָאִשָּׁה] into the house of Pharaoh [בֵּית פַּרְעֹה].

**12:16** And to Abram he did good [וּלְאַבְרָם הֵיטִיב] on her account [בַּעֲבוּרָהּ] and he had [וַיְהִי-לוֹ] flocks [צֹאן] and cattle [וּבָקָר] and he-donkeys [וַחֲמֹרִים] and male-servants [וַעֲבָדִים] and female-servants [וּשְׁפָחֹת] and she-donkeys [וַאֲתֹנֹת] and camels [וּגְמַלִּים].

**12:17** And the LORD afflicted [וַיְנַגַּע יְהוָה] Pharaoh [אֶת-פַּרְעֹה] and his household [וְאֶת-בֵּיתוֹ] with great plagues [נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים] on account of Sarai [עַל-דְּבַר שָׂרַי] the wife of Abram [אֵשֶׁת אַבְרָם].

**12:18** And Pharaoh called [וַיִּקְרָא פַרְעֹה] to Abram [לְאַבְרָם] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: What is this [מַה-זֹּאת] you have done to me [עָשִׂיתָ לִּי]? Why [לָמָּה] did you not tell me [לֹא-הִגַּדְתָּ לִּי] that she was your wife [כִּי אִשְׁתְּךָ הִוא]?

**12:19** Why did you say [לָמָּה אָמַרְתָּ]: She is my sister [אֲחֹתִי הִוא] and I took her [וָאֶקַּח אֹתָהּ] for myself as wife [לִי לְאִשָּׁה]? And now [וְעַתָּה] here is your wife [הִנֵּה אִשְׁתְּךָ] — take her [קַח] and go [וָלֵךְ].

**12:20** And Pharaoh commanded [וַיְצַו עָלָיו פַּרְעֹה] concerning him [עָלָיו] men [אֲנָשִׁים] and they escorted him [וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֹתוֹ] and his wife [וְאֶת-אִשְׁתּוֹ] and all that was his [וְאֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### On 12:1 — לֶךְ-לְךָ: "Go For Yourself"

**לֶךְ-לְךָ (lekh-lekha)** is the most discussed phrase in Genesis 12 and one of the most compressed theological expressions in the entire Hebrew Bible. The construction pairs the imperative of הָלַךְ (halakh, "to go, walk") with the dative reflexive pronoun לְךָ — literally "go to yourself" or "go for yourself."

**What does לְךָ add to לֶךְ?** The imperative alone (לֶךְ, "go!") would be sufficient for a simple command. The addition of לְךָ creates what grammarians call the **dative of advantage** or **dative of interest** — the going is *for Abram's own benefit, advantage, or fulfillment*. Several nuances are alive simultaneously:

1. **"Go for yourself"** — the journey benefits Abram; the destination is his destiny, not merely an obedient errand.
2. **"Go to yourself"** — a mystical reading (developed strongly in Hasidic interpretation, especially by the Baal Shem Tov and the Maggid of Mezeritch): the journey is an inward journey, a going into oneself. Lekh-lekha as the summons to self-discovery through displacement — only by leaving the familiar (land, kindred, father's house) can Abram discover who he truly is.
3. **The emphatic** — לְךָ adds rhetorical weight, functioning somewhat like the English "Go on!" or "Up and go!" — an emphatic imperative that brooks no delay.

**The threefold farewell — from greatest to smallest:**
The command strips Abram of three concentric layers of identity, listed *from the broadest to the most intimate*:
1. **מֵאַרְצְךָ (me'artsekha)** — from your land: national/territorial identity
2. **וּמִמּוֹלַדְתְּךָ (umimmoladetekha)** — from your kindred/birthplace: ethnic/clan identity (מוֹלֶדֶת is related to יָלַד, "to bear/beget" — the place/community of your birth)
3. **וּמִבֵּית אָבִיךָ (umibeit avikha)** — from the house of your father: the most intimate domestic identity

This movement from the general to the particular is rhetorically significant: psychologically, the hardest to leave is the last-named (father's house), not the first (the land). The order "land → kindred → father's house" heightens the emotional weight as it progresses — ending with the closest bond. The journey requires Abram to untether himself at every level of identity.

**Contrast with the LXX:**
LXX 12:1: **ἔξελθε ἐκ τῆς γῆς σου** (exelthe ek tes ges sou, "go out from your land"). The LXX uses a single verb ἔξελθε ("go out") and *loses* the reflexive/emphatic force of לְךָ entirely. The LXX rendering flattens לֶךְ-לְךָ into a plain departure command, making the theologically loaded repetition invisible in Greek. The manifest notes this explicitly: "The LXX at 12:1 renders lekh-lekha as ἔξελθε ἐκ ('go out from'), losing the emphatic doubling."

**Syntactical note:** The destination "to the land that I will show you" (אֶל-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אַרְאֶךָּ) is strikingly *indefinite* at the moment of the call. The land has no name. The Hebrew imperfect אַרְאֶךָּ ("I will show you") places the showing in the future — Abram departs toward a destination that God will disclose only as the journey unfolds. This is the paradigmatic act of faith: obedience toward an unnamed destination, trusting the word of the one who calls.

---

### On 12:2–3 — The Fivefold Blessing

These two verses contain the theological core of the entire Abrahamic covenant — what scholars call the **Urverheißung** (primordial promise). The structure unfolds in five discrete blessing-clauses, the last of which pivots from Abram to the nations:

**The five blessing statements:**
1. **וְאֶעֶשְׂךָ לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל** — "and I will make you into a great nation"
2. **וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ** — "and I will bless you"
3. **וַאֲגַדְּלָה שְׁמֶךָ** — "and I will make great your name"
4. **וֶהְיֵה בְּרָכָה** — "and be a blessing" (imperative — the single imperative in the series)
5. **וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה** — "and all the families of the earth shall bless themselves / be blessed in you"

**The structural pivot at clause 4:** Verses 2-3 alternate between what *God will do* (clauses 1-3, all first-person imperfects with waw-consecutive: "I will make... I will bless... I will make great") and what *Abram is to be or do* (clause 4, the imperative: **וֶהְיֵה בְּרָכָה**, "and be a blessing"). The single imperative in the series is pivotal: Abram is not merely the *recipient* of blessing but is commanded to *be* blessing — a conduit, a source, not merely a terminus.

**וַאֲגַדְּלָה שְׁמֶךָ ("I will make great your name"):** This clause directly reverses the Babel ambition (11:4: וְנַעֲשֶׂה-לָּנוּ שֵׁם, "let us make for ourselves a name"). At Babel, humanity attempts to *self-manufacture* greatness of name and fails; to Abram, YHWH *gives* greatness of name as a grace-gift. The Babel builders sought permanence through monumental construction; Abram will receive the same thing through trust and obedience. The narrative is explicitly designed to show these two as competing models of how a name becomes great.

**On the blessing/cursing asymmetry in v. 3:**
- **מְבָרְכֶיךָ** (plural): "those who bless you" — plural participle; those who bless Abram are many.
- **וּמְקַלֶּלְךָ** (singular): "the one who curses you" — singular participle; the curser is conceived as an exceptional individual case.
- **אָאֹר** ("I will curse"): the verb here is אָרַר (arar, "to curse"), which is distinct from the verb in the second pair (קָלַל, qalal, "to treat as light/slight, to curse").
- The grammar encodes an asymmetry: *blessing* is the normative response (plural — many will bless), while *cursing* is the aberrant exception (singular — the odd one who dares to curse).

---

### On 12:3 — וְנִבְרְכוּ: Niphal or Hithpael? "Be Blessed" or "Bless Themselves"?

**וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה** — the final and most expansive clause of the Urverheißung.

The form וְנִבְרְכוּ is the crux of the entire verse. The verb form can be parsed as:

1. **Niphal (passive):** "and all the families of the earth *shall be blessed* in you" — the blessing flows *to* the nations *through* Abram; they are the passive recipients of blessing mediated by him.

2. **Hithpael (reflexive):** "and all the families of the earth *shall bless themselves* in you" — the nations use Abram's name as the standard of blessing, saying "may you be like Abram" or invoking his name as the paradigm of the blessed life. The Hithpael of בָּרַךְ occurs in 22:18 and 26:4 (וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ) — the parallel passages make it clear the Hithpael reading is live and important.

**Textual evidence:**
- Genesis 12:3 uses **וְנִבְרְכוּ** — Niphal form.
- Genesis 18:18 uses **וְנִבְרְכוּ** — Niphal form (same).
- Genesis 22:18 and 26:4 use **וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ** — Hithpael form explicitly.
- Genesis 28:14 uses **וְנִבְרְכוּ** — Niphal form again.

The switching between Niphal (12:3; 18:18; 28:14) and Hithpael (22:18; 26:4) at the corresponding formulaic point in the Abrahamic promises is not accidental. The Niphal stresses the *objective mediation* of blessing — the nations receive it through Abram's line. The Hithpael stresses the *subjective use* of Abram's blessing as a formula — the nations invoke his name.

**Paul's reading (Galatians 3:8):** Paul quotes LXX Genesis 12:3 (ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν σοὶ πάντα τὰ ἔθνη, "all nations will be blessed in you") using a passive form — the passive/Niphal reading — and applies it to the gospel blessing flowing to the Gentiles through Christ as Abraham's seed. The LXX uses a passive future (ἐνευλογηθήσονται) which represents the Niphal reading.

**EMET translation policy:** The EMET renders "shall bless themselves" to preserve the Niphal/Hithpael ambiguity (the reflexive meaning is present in the Niphal as well as the Hithpael in Hebrew) and to flag that the nations are *agents* of their own blessing invocation, not merely passive recipients. A footnote alerts the reader to the passive reading.

**הָאֲדָמָה vs. הָאָרֶץ:** The final clause uses **הָאֲדָמָה** ("the ground/soil/earth" in a more intimate agricultural sense) rather than הָאָרֶץ (the more political/geographic "land/earth"). This is the word used in the creation narrative for the ground from which Adam was formed (2:7: אֲדָמָה). The "families of the earth" (מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה) are the families *of the ground* — the human communities rooted in the soil, the full extent of humanity as created beings of the earth. The word choice connects the Abrahamic promise back to the creation of humanity and forward to all human communities.

---

### On 12:4 — Abram's Age and the Chronological Note

**וְאַבְרָם בֶּן-חָמֵשׁ שָׁנִים וְשִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה בְּצֵאתוֹ מֵחָרָן — "and Abram was seventy-five years old when he went out from Haran."**

This chronological note is one of only three ages given for Abram in the patriarchal narrative (the others: 86 at Ishmael's birth, 13:16; 99 at the covenant of circumcision, 17:1). The age 75 is significant in several ways:

- It marks Abram as a mature man, past middle age even by patriarchal standards — God calls him not in the vigor of youth but in the maturity of an established life.
- Combined with Sarai's age (implied as 65, since she is 10 years younger per 17:17: "Sarai who is ninety" when Abraham is 100), it sets up the impossibility of childbearing: a 65-year-old barren woman (11:30) departing for a foreign land on the basis of a promise of nationhood.
- The connection to Terah's death (11:32): Terah died at 205; if Abram was born when Terah was 130 (as the MT chronology implies — see note on 11:26), then Abram is 75 when Terah dies at 205 (205 − 130 = 75). Acts 7:4 states Stephen's sermon ("after his father died, God moved him from there"), implying Abram waited in Haran until Terah died. The MT chronology makes this precise: Terah's death and Abram's departure are simultaneous.

**The LXX** (Codex Vaticanus, pa_0008) follows the MT at 75 years (ἑβδομήκοντα πέντε ἐτῶν) — no variant here.

---

### On 12:5 — הַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ בְחָרָן: "The Persons Whom They Had Made in Haran"

**וְאֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ בְחָרָן** — "and the persons whom they had made in Haran." This phrase is one of the most enigmatic in Genesis 12.

**הַנֶּפֶשׁ (hannefesh, "the persons/souls"):** נֶפֶשׁ in the singular can be collective ("the persons" = people). The phrase refers to members of Abram's household — slaves, retainers, servants acquired in Haran.

**אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ (asher-asu, "whom they made/acquired"):** The verb עָשָׂה (asah, "to make, do") is unusual for acquiring people — one normally "buys" (קָנָה) slaves or "takes" (לָקַח) servants. The use of "made" for persons is striking. Classical rabbinic interpretation (Midrash Rabbah, Rashi) understood this as referring to converts whom Abram and Sarai had *made* into monotheists — souls they had brought into the knowledge of YHWH. Rashi: "Abram would bring in men under the wings of the Shekhinah (Divine Presence)." The LXX renders ἃς ἐκτήσαντο ("which/whom they had acquired"), choosing a commercial term (κτάομαι, "to acquire, possess") that removes the creative/formative nuance of עָשָׂה.

The EMET renders "made" to preserve the Hebrew's unusual and theologically freighted word choice, even though "acquired" is the more prosaic sense.

---

### On 12:6 — וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי אָז בָּאָרֶץ: The Canaanite Was Then in the Land

**This parenthetical remark is one of the most significant textual and literary cruxes in Genesis.**

**The note reads:** "and the Canaanite was then in the land [אָז בָּאָרֶץ]." The word אָז (az, "then") is the key. It implies that at some later point of narration — the narrator's own present — the Canaanite was *no longer* in the land in the same way.

**Interpretive options:**

1. **The documentary-critical reading (de Wette, 1805; Wellhausen):** "Then the Canaanite was in the land" implies the narrator writes from a perspective *after* the Canaanite population had been displaced — i.e., after the Israelite conquest under Joshua. This is the observation that prompted de Wette's landmark thesis that the Pentateuch could not have been written by Moses (since Moses died before the conquest) — the anachronism of "the Canaanite was *then* in the land" suggests a post-conquest narrator. Wellhausen and the Documentary Hypothesis built on this: the J source was composed during the period of the monarchy, looking back.

2. **The traditional reading (pre-critical; Rashi, Ramban, Calvin):** "Then" simply marks historical contemporaneity — "at that time [when Abram was in the land], the Canaanite happened to be in the land." The note explains why the LORD's promise (v. 7: "to your seed I will give this land") was remarkable — the land was occupied. The promise was not for an empty land but for a presently-inhabited territory. This reading requires no post-conquest perspective.

3. **A literary aside marking foreignness:** The narrator, writing for an Israelite audience that knows the land as "their" land, uses the aside to mark the strangeness of Abram's sojourn in a land full of foreigners. Abram is the alien, the sojourner (גֵּר) in a land belonging to others — yet this is the land promised to his seed.

The EMET preserves the aside verbatim with the literal "the Canaanite was *then* in the land" — flagging the temporal marker אָז without resolving it. The word "then" is both historically and theologically live: then (not now), the Canaanite held what God had promised to Abram's seed.

**LXX 12:6:** ὁ δὲ Χαναναῖος τότε διεχώριζεν τὴν γῆν — "And the Canaanite was then dividing/occupying the land." The LXX uses τότε (tote, "then") faithfully for אָז, and διεχώριζεν ("was dividing/occupying") is an interpretive expansion of the simple Hebrew בָּאָרֶץ ("in the land") — the LXX perhaps emphasizing that the Canaanite controlled/apportioned the land at that time.

---

### On 12:7 — The First Divine Appearance (Theophany) to Abram

**וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָם** — "And the LORD appeared to Abram."

This is the first recorded theophany (divine appearance) to a patriarch in Genesis. The verb וַיֵּרָא (vayyera, Niphal of רָאָה, "to see") — "and he caused himself to be seen / and he appeared" — is the standard term for divine self-disclosure. The same verb forms the basis of the naming of Moriah in 22:14 (יְהוָה יִרְאֶה, "the LORD will see/provide") and appears at every major theophany in the patriarchal narratives (17:1; 18:1; 26:2, 24; 35:1, 9).

**The content of the appearance:** "To your seed I will give this land." This is the land promise — brief, unconditional, and future-oriented. Note:
- **לְזַרְעֲךָ (lezarakha, "to your seed"):** זֶרַע (zera', "seed") is singular-collective — it can mean one descendant or many. Paul exploits this in Galatians 3:16, arguing that "seed" (singular) refers to Christ specifically. In context, the collective sense is primary: Abram's *descendants* as a people will inherit the land.
- **אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת (et-ha'aretz hazzot, "this land"):** the demonstrative "this" — pointing to the very land Abram is now traversing as a stranger. The land he walks through as an alien will belong to his descendants.

**The altar-building:** Abram's response to each theophany or divine moment in the land is to build an altar — here (v. 7), at Bethel (v. 8), and later at Mamre (13:18). The altar-building is an act of worship and territorial consecration: Abram marks the sites of divine encounter with altars, planting stakes of sacred memory in the land of promise. This is the opposite of the Babel builders' tower: the tower was humanity's attempt to reach heaven by force; the altar is Abram's response to heaven's reaching down.

---

### On 12:8 — מִקֶּדֶם לְבֵית-אֵל: Bethel and Ai

**בֵּית-אֵל מִיָּם וְהָעַי מִקֶּדֶם** — "Bethel to the west and Ai to the east." The topographical precision here is notable: Bethel (modern Beitin) is the venerated site of Jacob's dream (28:10-22) — it will become a central sanctuary in the northern kingdom of Israel after the division. Ai (et-Tell) is the city attacked twice by Joshua (Joshua 7-8). The narrator positions Abram between these two future landmarks of Israelite history — he camps between the site of Jacob's theophany and the site of Joshua's first military failure and subsequent victory. The land's future history is mapped onto Abram's itinerary.

**וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה (vayyiqra veshem YHWH, "and called on the name of the LORD"):** The phrase קָרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה means to invoke the LORD by his covenant name — to worship, pray, and publicly declare his name. The same phrase is used for Noah's son Enosh (4:26: אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה, "then it was begun to call on the name of the LORD") and recurs throughout the patriarchal narratives as the signature act of covenant relationship. Abram's altar-building and name-calling transforms the land he travels through into a landscape of YHWH worship.

---

### On 12:9 — הָלוֹךְ וְנָסוֹעַ: The Infinitive Absolute Construction

**וַיִּסַּע אַבְרָם הָלוֹךְ וְנָסוֹעַ הַנֶּגְבָּה** — "And Abram journeyed on, going and journeying toward the Negev."

The paired infinitive absolutes **הָלוֹךְ וְנָסוֹעַ** ("going and journeying") create a picture of continuous movement — the same construction used in 8:3 (the waters receding after the flood: הָלוֹךְ וָשׁוֹב, "going and returning"). The construction emphasizes the progressive, stage-by-stage nature of Abram's southward journey. He does not rush; he moves methodically, camping and moving, altar by altar, toward the Negev (the dry south) — and eventually, pressed by famine (v. 10), down into Egypt.

**הַנֶּגְבָּה (hanegbah):** The Negev is the arid southland of Canaan — semi-desert territory south of Hebron, transitioning toward the Sinai wilderness. The direction "toward the Negev" (with the directional heh suffix) anticipates the Egyptian detour: from the Negev it is natural to continue south into Egypt during drought.

---

### On 12:10–20 — The Wife-Sister Deception: Literary and Moral Analysis

This episode is one of the most morally complex in Genesis. It is told three times in the patriarchal narratives:
- Genesis 12:10-20: Abram/Sarai in Egypt before Pharaoh
- Genesis 20:1-18: Abraham/Sarah before Abimelech of Gerar (with a theological explanation added: Sarah is technically Abraham's half-sister, 20:12)
- Genesis 26:1-11: Isaac/Rebekah before Abimelech of Gerar (Rebekah is clearly not Isaac's sister — the deception is even more transparent)

**The literary function of the repetition:** The three-fold recurrence of the wife-sister motif is a classic biblical pattern of type-scenes (cf. Robert Alter's analysis of the "betrothal at the well" type-scene in Genesis 24, 29, Exodus 2). The repetition creates a pattern that invites comparison: each iteration reveals something different about the characters and about divine protection.

**Moral analysis of 12:10-20:**

Abram's behavior in this episode is morally troubling by any standard:
1. **He uses Sarai as a shield for his own safety** (v. 13: "say you are my sister... so that my life shall live because of you"). Her safety is not his stated concern; his own survival is.
2. **He profits materially from the deception** (v. 16: Pharaoh gives him livestock and servants). Abram acquires wealth directly from the lie.
3. **Sarai is taken into Pharaoh's house** (v. 15: וַתֻּקַּח הָאִשָּׁה, "and the woman was taken") — the passive verb and the absence of any recorded speech or resistance from Sarai is deliberately stark.
4. **YHWH intervenes to protect Sarai** (v. 17), not by preventing the deception but by afflicting Pharaoh — YHWH acts to protect the promise (seed through Sarai) even when Abram himself endangers it.
5. **Pharaoh's moral indignation** (vv. 18-19) is entirely justified: "Why did you not tell me she was your wife?" The pagan king rebukes the patriarch of the covenant. This is one of several passages in Genesis where outsiders display greater ethical sensitivity than the covenant people.

**The narrator's silence:** The narrator offers no direct moral evaluation. There is no divine rebuke of Abram. The story simply narrates what happened and what its consequences were. This withholding of judgment is characteristic of Genesis's narrative technique — the reader must sit with the moral discomfort.

---

### On 12:10 — רָעָב: Famine as Narrative Driver

**וַיְהִי רָעָב בָּאָרֶץ** — "And there was a famine in the land." Famine is a recurring narrative pivot in Genesis: it drives Jacob and his sons to Egypt (42:1-2: "Jacob saw that there was grain in Egypt... for the famine was heavy in the land of Canaan"). The structural parallel between Genesis 12 and Genesis 42-47 is deliberate: in both cases, the patriarch goes down to Egypt under the pressure of famine; in both cases, God orchestrates events from within the crisis to advance the promise; in both cases, the patriarch (Abram / Joseph's family) leaves Egypt with increased wealth. The typology is: Abram's Egypt episode prefigures the Egyptian sojourn of Israel.

**לָגוּר שָׁם (lagur sham, "to sojourn there"):** The verb גּוּר (gur, "to sojourn, dwell as a stranger") is the root of גֵּר (ger, "sojourner/resident alien") — a key legal and ethical category in Israelite law. Abram goes to Egypt not to settle permanently but to sojourn — he remains a foreigner, a temporary resident. The law of Moses will repeatedly command care for the גֵּר "because you were sojourners in the land of Egypt" (e.g., Lev. 19:34; Deut. 10:19) — the Egyptian sojourn, foreshadowed here in Abram's brief visit, becomes the founding ethical rationale for Israelite care of the alien.

---

### On 12:11 — יְפַת-מַרְאֶה: Beautiful in Appearance

**כִּי אִשָּׁה יְפַת-מַרְאֶה אָתְּ** — "that you are a woman beautiful in appearance." יְפַת-מַרְאֶה is the standard biblical epithet for female beauty — the same phrase used of Rachel (29:17) and Esther (Esth. 2:7). Sarai is 65 years old at this point (Abraham is 75 and she is 10 years younger) — the text's assertion of her beauty at this age is either a feature of the extended patriarchal lifespan (beauty and vitality lasting proportionally longer) or a literary idealization. The narrator does not explain; he states it as fact.

**The speech as foreshadowing:** Abram's speech to Sarai before entering Egypt (vv. 11-13) is the only recorded direct speech between husband and wife in the entire Abram narrative until the Sarah/Hagar conflict (16:6). The content of this first conjugal exchange — a husband instructing his wife to conceal their marriage for his own protection — is starkly unpromising. Yet it is the opening of the wife-sister narrative, and the marriage survives it. The intimacy of Abram's address "Look, I know..." (הִנֵּה-נָא יָדַעְתִּי) carries a note of weary pragmatism: he has assessed the danger and constructed a plan.

---

### On 12:13 — אֲחֹתִי אָתְּ: The Technical Defense

**אִמְרִי-נָא אֲחֹתִי אָתְּ** — "Say, please, you are my sister." In Genesis 20:12, Abraham defends himself before Abimelech by explaining that Sarah is indeed his half-sister: "Moreover she is truly my sister — she is the daughter of my father though not the daughter of my mother, and she became my wife." Whether this defense is also operative here (Sarai is already his half-sister before the Egyptian episode) is left unstated in chapter 12 — the reader is simply given the deception and must wait for chapter 20's partial clarification.

**לְמַעַן יִיטַב-לִי (lema'an yitav-li, "so that it may go well with me"):** The phrase is damagingly honest — Abram's stated motivation is entirely self-interested: his welfare. The moral irony sharpens in v. 16: it *does* go well with him materially, immediately, precisely because of the deception (וּלְאַבְרָם הֵיטִיב בַּעֲבוּרָהּ, "and to Abram he did good on her account"). The Hebrew root יָטַב echoes from Abram's desire (12:13) to its fulfillment (12:16) — the deception works, in the most narrow sense, exactly as planned.

---

### On 12:16 — The Livestock List and the Camels

**צֹאן וּבָקָר וַחֲמֹרִים וַעֲבָדִים וּשְׁפָחֹת וַאֲתֹנֹת וּגְמַלִּים** — "flocks and cattle and he-donkeys and male-servants and female-servants and she-donkeys and camels."

The list is comprehensive and climactic — the accumulation of wealth that will define Abram/Abraham as a great patriarch (cf. 13:2: "And Abram was very heavy/rich in livestock, in silver, and in gold"). The Hebrew word order separates the animals and the human servants and then adds the camels last — camels are the most prestigious and valuable of the animals listed.

**The camels — an archaeological crux:** The mention of camels (גְּמַלִּים, gemalim) in the patriarchal narratives has long been contested. Domesticated camels do not appear in reliably dated Egyptian or Canaanite archaeological records before approximately the 10th-9th centuries BCE — the patriarchal period (traditionally ca. 2000-1700 BCE) predates the widespread domestication of the camel in the ancient Near East by several centuries. This anachronism (if it is one) is cited as evidence that the patriarchal narratives received their literary form in the Iron Age rather than the Bronze Age. Defenders of early authenticity point to scattered earlier references and the possibility of limited early camel domestication. The EMET notes this without resolving it: the text says camels, and camels it is.

---

### On 12:17 — נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים: Great Plagues

**וַיְנַגַּע יְהוָה אֶת-פַּרְעֹה וְאֶת-בֵּיתוֹ נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים** — "And the LORD afflicted Pharaoh and his household with great plagues."

**נֶגַע (nega', "plague/affliction/stroke"):** The same root is used for the Egyptian plagues in Exodus (the verbal form in Exod. 12:23 and elsewhere). The word can mean disease, skin affliction, or divine blow/stroke. Here, in the story of Abram in Egypt, the LORD afflicts Pharaoh with "great plagues" (נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים) — the same Pharaoh and Egypt that will later suffer the Ten Plagues in the Exodus. The connection is deliberate and typological: Abram's Egyptian episode is a miniature Exodus — patriarch goes down to Egypt, is threatened, YHWH afflicts Pharaoh, patriarch leaves with great wealth. The formula "great plagues" echoes across the canon.

**The mechanism of Pharaoh's knowledge:** The text does not explain how Pharaoh learned (v. 18) that Sarai was Abram's wife — presumably through the nature of the afflictions (perhaps specifically a disease that prevented marital relations) or through a divine communication. The LXX adds no clarification. The narrative simply presents Pharaoh confronting Abram with full knowledge — the deception exposed, presumably by divine agency.

**עַל-דְּבַר שָׂרַי (al-davar Sarai, "on account of Sarai"):** The phrase דָּבַר ("word/matter/account") used here is also the word for "matter/thing/word." YHWH's affliction is "on the word of Sarai" — her situation, her cause, her person is what moves YHWH to act. She is the object of divine protection even when she is not the subject of human speech. Her silence in this narrative is conspicuous; her protection by YHWH is its eloquent answer.

---

### On 12:18-19 — Pharaoh's Rebuke

**מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לִּי** — "What is this you have done to me?" This exact formula of moral rebuke recurs throughout Genesis: Isaac to Abimelech (26:10: "What is this you have done to us?"), Jacob to Laban (31:26: "What have you done?"), Judah to his brothers (44:4: "Why have you repaid evil for good?"). It is the standard formula for moral confrontation. Here, the pagan king Pharaoh becomes the first person in the patriarchal narrative to rebuke Abram — and the rebuke is unanswerable. The text gives Abram no response. He is escorted out in silence (v. 20).

**The absence of Abram's reply** is as significant as anything Abram says elsewhere. His silence before Pharaoh's rebuke is the narrative's own judgment: Abram has no defense to offer. The deception is exposed, the damage acknowledged, and the patriarch departs without vindication. The story does not rehabilitate him here. The rehabilitation comes not through moral argument but through the continuing faithfulness of YHWH to his promises despite the patriarch's failures.

---

### On 12:20 — וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֹתוֹ: The Sending Away

**וַיְצַו עָלָיו פַּרְעֹה אֲנָשִׁים וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֹתוֹ** — "And Pharaoh commanded concerning him men and they escorted him."

The verb שָׁלַח (shalach, "to send away, release, escort") used here will echo loudly in Exodus: "Send my people that they may serve me" (שַׁלַּח אֶת-עַמִּי, Exod. 5:1) — the demand of Moses to the later Pharaoh. Here the direction is reversed: Pharaoh sends Abram *out* of Egypt (the proto-Exodus); later Pharaoh must be compelled to send Israel *out* of Egypt (the Exodus proper). Both events leave Egypt richer for the patriarchal family: here, Abram leaves with Pharaoh's gifts (v. 16); later, Israel leaves with the "plunder of Egypt" (Exod. 12:36). The typological parallelism is exact.

**The camels, the servants, the wealth** — all that Abram acquired in Egypt he carries back to Canaan (13:1-2). The narrative continues directly into Genesis 13, where Abram's wealth creates a separation problem with Lot, resolved by Abram's gracious choice to let Lot choose his portion of the land. The Egypt episode thus functions as the crucible that both tests the patriarch and provides the material foundation for the next phase of the narrative.

---

## LXX Comparison Summary for Genesis 12

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Codex Vaticanus) | Notes |
|-------|---------------------|----------------------|-------|
| 12:1 | לֶךְ-לְךָ ("go for yourself") | ἔξελθε ἐκ ("go out from") | LXX loses the emphatic reflexive lekh-lekha |
| 12:3 | וְנִבְרְכוּ ("shall bless themselves/be blessed," Niphal) | ἐνευλογηθήσονται ("shall be blessed," passive) | LXX adopts the passive/Niphal reading; cf. Gal. 3:8 |
| 12:5 | אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ ("whom they made") | ἃς ἐκτήσαντο ("which they acquired") | LXX normalizes the unusual "made" for people |
| 12:6 | בָּאָרֶץ ("in the land") | διεχώριζεν τὴν γῆν ("was dividing/occupying the land") | LXX expands MT's plain "in the land" |
| 12:7 | וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה ("the LORD appeared") | ὤφθη κύριος ("the LORD appeared") | LXX agrees with MT; standard theophany formula |
| 12:8 | מִקֶּדֶם לְבֵית-אֵל ("east of Bethel") | κατὰ ἀνατολάς Βαιθηλ ("toward the east of Bethel") | Minor prepositional difference; LXX agrees in substance |
| 12:10 | כִּי-כָבֵד הָרָעָב ("for the famine was heavy") | ἐνίσχυσεν γὰρ ὁ λιμός ("for the famine grew strong") | LXX uses "grew strong" for MT "was heavy" |
| 12:16 | גְּמַלִּים ("camels") listed | κάμηλοι ("camels") listed | LXX agrees; no variant on the camels |
| 12:17 | נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים ("great plagues") | ἐτάσεσιν μεγάλαις ("great afflictions/torments") | LXX uses ἐτάσεις rather than πληγαί for נְגָעִים; different word for "plagues" |
| 12:18 | מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לִּי ("what is this you have done to me?") | τί τοῦτο ἐποίησάς μοι ("what is this you have done to me?") | LXX agrees exactly with MT in the rebuke formula |
| 12:19 | כִּי אִשְׁתְּךָ הִוא ("that she was your wife") | ὅτι γυνή μού ἐστιν ("that she is my wife") | LXX switches to first person (Pharaoh's perspective) vs. MT's second person (Abram addressed) |

---

## Dead Sea Scrolls, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Versional Notes

**Dead Sea Scrolls — Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen):** Genesis 12 is extensively retold in the Genesis Apocryphon from Qumran Cave 1 (columns XIX-XX), which represents the most expanded retelling of any patriarchal narrative in the DSS corpus. The Apocryphon notably:
- Adds a dream (col. XIX) in which Abram sees a cedar tree (himself) and a palm tree (Sarai) — men come to cut down the cedar but the palm pleads for the cedar's survival; Abram interprets this as a premonition of the Egyptian danger.
- Expands Sarai's beauty with a lengthy poetic description read as a poem of praise (cols. XX:2-8) — one of the few surviving ancient Hebrew poems of feminine beauty, in which her face, forehead, eyes, nose, cheeks, lips, neck, hands, and feet are all enumerated.
- Names Pharaoh's advisor who praises Sarai to Pharaoh as **Harkenosh**.
- Describes Pharaoh's affliction explicitly as inability to approach Sarai sexually (the "plagues" in the MT are unspecified; the Apocryphon makes them sexually incapacitating).
- Has Lot intercede with Abram on Pharaoh's behalf before Abram prays and heals Pharaoh.

These expansions are not canonical but attest to the intense interest this narrative generated in Second Temple Judaism. No text-critical variants from 1QapGen affect the MT of Genesis 12.

**Samaritan Pentateuch:** The SP agrees with the MT throughout Genesis 12 without significant variants. The SP text of the Abrahamic call and the Egypt episode tracks the MT consistently.

**Targum Onkelos:** Renders straightforwardly; at 12:3 Onkelos translates וְנִבְרְכוּ as "shall be blessed" (passive), following the Niphal reading.

**Targum Pseudo-Jonathan:** Adds the information that Sarai was taken into Pharaoh's house for the night but that Pharaoh could not approach her because of the divine protection. The "plagues" are expanded to include a skin affliction that prevented him from touching her.

**Josephus (Antiquities I.8.1):** Josephus retells the Egypt episode, adding that Abram taught the Egyptians arithmetic and astronomy during his sojourn — a legendary elaboration designed to show the patriarch as a benefactor of civilization rather than merely a fearful deceiver.
